author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Yep, constructing and upgrading buildings will be there at release. But for the current sfts of these buildings we might have to convert them to the resources needed to craft them or have a special case to allow those specific tokens to be used. Because the weirdness happens where you have to move these units to their location wherever your claim stake is. And it's just awkward to put a whole building in a ship. But we haven't conclusively planned out how to reconcile these, but they will be honored and give the full capability of the equivalent final asset of those buildings. also, mining drills won't be required for all claimstakes, there will be other things to do on plots than mine. and there will be minimal power in the central hub that comes with the CS so you only need the power plant if you're going to expand beyond the power usage of the first hub.   Glad you're enjoying Galia man, part of season 0 roadmap right now is to figure out outposts for mini games on the planet, which is a stepping stone to player generated outposts aka claim stakes.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Basically that whole system is deprecated. The way the system works is that the CS can be capitalized on to a degree by just building the hubs and upgrading those. Central, extraction, processing, farm and storage hubs. But for advanced plots each of those hubs have modules that can be built and upgraded for deeper configuration and optimization of the plot. We have a lot of modules but are still fine tuning the total amount. They’re all considered buildings in this system and each tier is also considered a building so technically our building count is in the hundreds. Should have it nailed down fairly soon though to where we’ll feel confident to share more.

author: questionoak
content: We really need to default SAGE to NOT "load other fleets". I just rebooted my computer twice. Went through apps I had installed recently and uninstalled them. Only to determine that because I had cleared and reset session data on SAGE, my computer was crawling. It is flying again, but that was frustrating to waste time trying to find a solution when we know this option does not work well. My laptop is probably more powerful than most with 36GB and a 4090. Hate to know how slow other computers run with that option turned on.  I guess it also makes a lot of unnessecary RPC requests as well to get all that data. That is just money wasted from the DAO in my opinion.

author: The Last DayUstur
content: I think ships are just one variable in the equation; it all revolves around the number of users. Doubling the ships for the same player base could be an issue, just like having a deflationary ecosystem where there aren’t enough ships. We need balance. I believe that’s pretty much why we still can’t craft ships, but we will in the future—as soon as we have an appropriate player base. Just my two cents.

An active player will never rent out his fleet.,Another player who dealt with sagelabs, bought a team and created a fleet, will also not lease it, he will prefer to extract hydrogen..,In fact, it turns out that this section, unfortunately, will only be for "rare exceptions" who have strained to create a fleet, buy a crew, understand the work of sagelabs, but decided to lease it at a FAVORABLE price to the tenant. 3.1. At an adequate price, "rare characters" will not be able to set a price because they do not know it. The Atlas price changes all the time, it doesn't play, I can't adjust it without additional information. Therefore, prices will always be inadequate. Which will make this section unprofitable.

author: [Shai-Hulud] ToyityUstur
content: BTW, the big win on this come from attracting an "investor" class to Star Atlas. People that have no intention of playing the game, but identify game assets as a passive income opportunity. Much like investing in real estate rentals. And that will happen if demand exists on the rental platform. It will drive ship prices back up to origination as assets get acquired for rentals, help ATMTA sell more new ships and scale our team, and also still accomplish the goal of providing lower cost entry to new users in SA.

content: The Owner, who is renting out the fleet, pays 10% of their revenue to the platform. This is split across multiple parties. author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
As always, I’m not able to give a reliable opinion on the economic side of things, but I think I have a solid grasp of the gaming aspect. Having an HRZ with a permanent loss of assets implies that you need to restrict that area to a very small portion of the map compared to the other two sections.

90% of players won’t even consider going into the HRZ, and you’ll end up with a "dead zone." I assume you’ll encounter some very dangerous NPCs in the HRZ, so asset loss won’t come only from other players but also from alien behemoths that you’ll have to raid with your DAC or other players.

I’m not for or against this feature—I honestly don’t care. I just want the game to be fun. I’m simply presenting the possibilities and what could happen depending on which path you choose!

Back in the day, games like Dark Orbit had only 3 PvP maps out of 15 total, and you couldn’t leave if you were engaged in combat or constantly running from an enemy. PvP is fun, but it’s also stressful. It creates engagement and a sense of competition, but it also pushes casual players away from the game. We need balance.

PS: I'm just chatting, everything is up to team decision, I just like to talk about gaming 

PPS: Ships are fun, but this is not just a ship game—making SA a ship-only experience would turn it into a niche game. You have to consider everything. Many players want to explore in FPS/TPS mode, walking, running, and shooting. We should look at the bigger picture.
I imagine the HRZ as something similar to Helldivers 2, for example—a planet full of enemies that you have to defend against to obtain rare materials. (And maybe some empty planets with harsh living conditions, like extreme temperatures or low atmosphere—just an idea!)

content: 600$ mining fleet (2ximp tips) need 145crew to be able to craft (food upkeep) and install (tools) frames, they are able to make from iron they mined. Not taking any sides, im ok with both keeping current numbers or lowering it, just wanted to point Chris was not so wrong with his math

author: DominicVainMod
content: I fully understand what he's talking about. Maybe everyone understands.  But obviously the way he talks is controversial.  But on the other hand, we need to solve something. Our goal is not just thousands of players.   Many users have been raising the issue for a long time, but it has not been solved clearly.  Whatever it is (lack of manpower, lack of team capabilities, etc.), this would be a fatal cause for users to leave the game.  It can be solved with automation tools, but the team shouldn't say that, because the team is not responsible for third-party tools.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
Team has already said they want to make improvements and that this initial version was an MVP. Getting the smart contracts right is 1000% smarter than having the "perfect" UI. Hell I would argue that the UI is doing its job exactly right. People are able to identify good deals and bad deals, full stop.

You guys need to stop automatically assuming team is bad because you don't like X feature or disagree with decision Y.

In 99.99% of cases team is very aware but they are choosing to do or not do X or Y because of limited resources, e.g. time and manpower.

Give them some credit. And time.

You could take this to extreme lengths -- a tool that also shows you what the estimated profit would be for what fleet and which types of proposed game loops to do with them, or several fleets combined. author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I thought I was abundantly clear on the town hall, brews, and in my written communication that this product was going to be launched in a very rough MVP state so we could get it out and test usage. This was absolutely not the top priority of the team, and in fact was a combined effort across multiple teams. In fact, our team wasn't even really involved in the UI. I had the choice of getting something released early (now), or holding it back until probably end of the year to get appropriate resources from our team on it for thorough review and revision. I wanted to get this out, and we're already seeing a ton of traction with it. Which is great. We know it can be (much) better, and we'll continue working on improving it. But my expectation was that people understood this was intentionally released in a rough state, given our focus on C4 and any other of a dozen projects we have ongoing.

I wanted to get this out, and we're already seeing a ton of traction with it. Which is great. We know it can be (much) better, and we'll continue working on improving it. But my expectation was that people understood this was intentionally released in a rough state, given our focus on C4 and any other of a dozen projects we have ongoing.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Thanks for the detailed response, Michael! I share the considerations you’ve made, as I had already mentioned to Bryan regarding waiting for the right moment. However, I want to reiterate that my concern—certainly unfounded—is that the ships could still be sold. Even though Star Atlas is not an easy “game”, many users use this complexity as an excuse not to learn the game rules.  I say this because, from the very first day of the crew release, we saw many packs for sale that could only have come from the drop meant for SCORE players. It’s equally true that they could put the ships up for rent without a crew (and without components/modules in the future).  I didn’t recall that the percentage had reached 33% of the initial issuance—I’ve been away from SCORE for too long and had no idea it had reached that level. Thanks for the clarification.  To conclude, my comment is just a consideration aimed at making Galia as populated as possible, certainly not a sterile critique or an attempt to benefit myself, as has been suggested.  Thanks, as always, for your patience and your valuable time!

reply.preview: Thanks for the detailed response, Michael! I share the considerations you’ve made, as I had already mentioned to Bryan regarding waiting for the right moment. I say this because, from the very first day of the crew release, we saw many packs for sale that could only have come from the drop meant for SCORE players. It’s equally true that they could put the ships up for rent without a crew (and without components/modules in the future). I didn’t recall that the percentage had reached 33% of the initial issuance—I’ve been away from SCORE for too long and had no idea it had reached that level. Thanks for the clarification. To conclude, my comment is just a consideration aimed at making Galia as populated as possible, certainly not a sterile critique or an attempt to benefit myself, as has been suggested. Thanks, as always, for your patience and your valuable time!

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: Our goals are the same. We still have a lot of work to do to get that very clean, simple onboarding experience. Especially given the sheer vastness of features available. Especially when you consider just how many products/features we've shipped over the years. It won't be easy consolidating all of that, and making everything intuitive. But we'll get there. And we need to retain the depth, which can be seen in things like @Ryden's Eveeye platform (wen AtlasEye?). That depth provides people with deep experiences to continuously engage in without getting bored.   For now, however, I'm most interested in getting the raw features out to market and letting people either figure it out, or have community programs step up to fill the gap in knowledge. It's part of the decentralized ethos. It's a good thing to not only permit, but encourage this external contribution to what ATMTA is building. If you have to wait for us to exclusively build everything to final polished shape, you'll be waiting years between releases and decades for a final product. We don't have that luxury of time, and the community contributors are doing an awesome job stepping in.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
Just imagine as the tools and UI get better humans will get even faster at accepting rentals. I’m not a huge fan of bid wars and it’d be annoying to see a fleet I want to accept disappear because I was out bid even though I was first.

Eveeye is lagged when it comes to its data. Even so if you have 60 people evenly spaced checking you get someone looking every second. Now what if there’s 600 people.

Private listing and DAC will help. Also you could technically bundle the create contract and accept rental to make it impossible to snipe.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: The trick is getting the lender to sign the create contract followed by the borrower signing the accept rental before submitting the two

author: bunthius [STAR]
content: @bunthius would be best to describe the visualization of fleets within UE (MMO vs Fleet Command). But I do believe you should be able to visualize all of the ships within a fleet, even if you're in the MMO format of the game. But you'll be limited to controlling a single ship directly. Or swapping between captains on the fly, which is likely less efficient. Otherwise, you will also have the ability to provide fleet commands for actions on a large scale via Fleet Command -- which is effectively just going to be our "map" view

author: 𝐋ᴀɴᴢᴇʀ [SPS]
content: Here are the ATLAS emissions for each star base upgrade.   | T2 | 7,000   |  | T3 | 14,500  | | T4 | 22,500  | | T5 | 31,250  |  For example, Oni currently have five tier 2 star base's, and one tier 5 star base. The base emission rate is 2,000,000 ATLAS. So the emission rate after upgrades should be;  2,000,000 + (7,000 *5) + 31,250 =  2,066,250 ATLAS.

author: 𝐋ᴀɴᴢᴇʀ [SPS]
content: That's a good question.  There's 2 camps:  Multi-faction:  have your members be in multiple factions.,Obvious weakness is: how are they going to manage attacking and defending each other?  If MUD gets attacked by some faction members, what are they going to do?  Blow up members of their faction?  Possible advantage:  better intra-faction trades down the line?  Single-faction:,Strength: everyone is focused and aligned. Potential weakness:  much harder to create intra-faction trade routes within the guild.  Personally I never thought multi-faction made sense which is one of the reasons I joined ROME.

author: [COEX] NDR
content: No, it's a known Solflare problem, check the pinned list of common problems/solutions (in the SLY discord). Quote: > is "last sign time" (see Tools->Statistics->in the header) larger than 2 seconds? If yes: Open Solflare, wait until the Solflare window shows your balances and all icons, close the window. Sign time should be normal again. You should do this every few days. It's a known Solflare bug/problem. The more fleets you have, the more often you need to do this.

author: Lorddo [ALRT]
content: If you accept my opinion here, I think with that fleet size you can still find you place in the game economy, you just have to deal with the fact FIC are not the way to go.  You can make profitable some game loop that whales cannot, they are some kind of Market Makers right now so they HAVE TO take part into FIC and LP.  If you still want to do that, I suggest to go full mining for a really high quantity, then you rent a big freight fleet for a day or two and you move what you mined in those 1-2 days without having to actually buy freighters. But again, you can actually have a higher profit margin than whales with some game loops which are not profitable for them but that work for you!

@[HEIM] SDU pimp  This discussion is happening because the devs have just changed the mechanics in favor of whales own ends ) 

The game is under development, and the mechanics are just being created and tested, and we are trying to find a balance - including here, sharing our opinions and conclusions.

How many whale players are in the game? 3-5%? This means that ~5% of players now have access to LP mechanics, and the remaining 95% (in your opinion) should sell SDU on the market at a modest price (most likely to the same whales)

I'm not saying that it should be done as before - of course, it was too easy. But the latest changes are too hard for most players. It would be nice if other players (including you) could also participate in the LP cycle.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: The LP system is for whales. It's literally only beneficial for whales, because the market lacks liquidity for that kind of volume. in terms of pure profit/dollar spent, LP is a self-cannibalizing system that whales are forced to use, because they won't get liquidity any other way. smaller players are making more money by either just selling to the whales, or selling on the market. that is my honest assessment (as someone who used to farm ~500m LP daily before, and stopped)

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
Disagree that FICs/LP are "whale" only.  Not even close.  As long as a player can mine and has transports, they can complete any FIC or LP loop.   It's really just a skill issue.

Let me give an example (numbers are placeholders).  Lets say I have a Mamba Ex mining Carbon.  Carbon has a value of 50 when sold on the open market.  Carbon in the FIC loop has a value of 75.   A player can choose to sell them on the market for 50.  Or, they can choose to engage in the FIC loop for 75.  But how do they unlock that 75 value?  By BUYING everything else in the chain. 

Let's flesh that out with Vaor Fics.

Carbon 50
Copper 40
Arco 100
Hydrogen 20
SDU 115

Right, so as a player in order to unlock that 50% carbon profit, I have to unlock the rest of the chain.  Copper, arco, hydrogen, SDUs.   As a small player I can't do all of those loops.   So I'll market buy them.  And thats pretty easy for small players because they dont need much, liquidity is not an issue.
So I buy everything, then craft my FICS, and redeem them.  Boom, I got all of the money back I used to purchase the raw materials AND I got 75 value from carbon that I mined.  PURE profit.  Anything you mine at this point is free money.  And of course there are costs associated with crafting and transport, so lets assume you only get 65 value back.  A modest 30% gain.  Not bad.  And the best part?

A small player can turn those profits into chipping away at all the items they have to buy. It's a roadmap for PURE PROFIT.  Or if they don't want to do that, they can double down on more Carbon and continue buying more raws to craft FICS.

What I'm actually seeing is just a lot of complaining.  For two reasons.

1) Players have unbalanced fleets which makes them not good at certain aspects of the loops.  e.g. Not enough scanners or not enough transports.  The solution is to either buy more, or sell off your current fleet to balance.  If a player wants a 100% completion of a loop, e.g. not market buying anything, simply do it. It's all there for the taking.  Sell your current ships or buy more.

2) Players want profits without working for it.  Anyone in the LP or FIC loop are GRINDING.  It's difficult, it requires a lot of strategy and logistics.  And it's pretty time consuming.   If you don't want to do any of those things, then simply sell your raws on the market.  There is absolutely NOTHING wrong with that.  I started my empire by mining carbon. I was the Carbon King. EMBRACE YOUR STRENGTHS.  Don't demand profits from game loops where you aren't willing to put out the work.

So I buy everything, then craft my FICS, and redeem them. Boom, I got all of the money back I used to purchase the raw materials AND I got 75 value from carbon that I mined. PURE profit. Anything you mine at this point is free money. And of course there are costs associated with crafting and transport, so lets assume you only get 65 value back. A modest 30% gain. Not bad. And the best part? A small player can turn those profits into chipping away at all the items they have to buy. It's a roadmap for PURE PROFIT. Or if they don't want to do that, they can double down on more Carbon and continue buying more raws to craft FICS. What I'm actually seeing is just a lot of complaining. For two reasons. 1) Players have unbalanced fleets which makes them not good at certain aspects of the loops. e.g. Not enough scanners or not enough transports. The solution is to either buy more, or sell off your current fleet to balance. If a player wants a 100% completion of a loop, e.g. not market buying anything, simply do it. It's all there for the taking. Sell your current ships or buy more. 2) Players want profits without working for it. Anyone in the LP or FIC loop are GRINDING. It's difficult, it requires a lot of strategy and logistics. And it's pretty time consuming. If you don't want to do any of those things, then simply sell your raws on the market. There is absolutely NOTHING wrong with that. I started my empire by mining carbon. I was the Carbon King. EMBRACE YOUR STRENGTHS. Don't demand profits from game loops where you aren't willing to put out the work.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
Have you ever played to Palworld? I mean, its pretty meh as a game, but I imagine NPC (Crew members specifically) work for you just like that. You go yourself to MRZ and HRZ and you let your NPCs play for you in LRZ. That may be considered automation and not botting.. its not WHAT you do but HOW you do, with all pros and cons.

Rust is a limited time game, its not inteded to keep going forever, at some point you have a server wipe and you start all over again, I think that's the fun part. Star Atlas is the opposite, it is meant to persist pretty much forever and independently from the players and the devs. I really see what you mean here but its like comparing apple to oranges.

We dont know yet how the team prototyped it and especially, we/they are building while riding it, there will be adjustments on the way 100%.

author: [COEX] 𝙈𝙪𝙖𝙙'𝙙𝙞𝙗
content: There needs to be some limited “automation” in UE5 whatever your definition of it. There should be an option for a player to perform a “mine this asteroid” action and have the option to do the whole task manually for a bonus to resources. Forcing everything to be manual just because it’s UE5 doesn’t make sense from a fun gameplay perspective. I personally think that most people would want to have some mundane tasks be automated and save mental attention span to more exciting aspects of the game like PvE, exploration and PvP. PvP will clear up bots/automated tasks in UE5 as there will be a risk to running an automated task like transport here etc.

author: Icy
content: Nope. Automation emerged on week one of Labs. Not as fancy as now. But people immediately started using auto-clickers, due to the fact that things were super fast, and repeatable.  It was absolutely accounted for 24/7 extraction, hence why the underlying econ for Sage worked from day 1. They knew it would happen, and from day one didn't condemn it. Quite the opposite. Automation got an award at the Naabathon, for example.  It's not up for debate, like it or not. Sage is accounting for automation since day 1.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
I like where @DominicVain his head is at. I fully agree we will need to be as on top of cheating as we possibly can.

I don’t think KYC, or max player caps in the HRZ are the answer though. I feel like we should be able to come up with some pretty cool software and tools that would be able to automatically detect a good chunk of the cheating that would affect the economy.

One approach I was thinking off to give humans more time to react is to limit the transfer of items obtained in the HRZ form of an hour or more, except if a SA program becomes the new owner (“semi-soulbound” certificates? )

Another thing I am always wondering about is a solution such as MagicBlock introduced with their ephemeral L2. What if we would expand on that and make an L2 that is ephemeral between a user (UE5) and a SA server. Then we could automatically analyze the incoming transactions (some in real-time, most right after in bundles) and write vetted summaries to Solana (also potentially allowing improved detection algorithms to have another go in the future).

Perhaps this also would allow for hidden movement and the like. 
I haven’t done the research, clearly, so may be way off here 

@Buzzec Would love to hear your thoughts on the above if you have any (and have time)

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
It depends on what you define as "cheating." As long as it’s not something disruptive to other players’ experience—whether economically or in terms of entertainment—cheating can be tolerated to some extent.

For example, using night vision in areas where PvP isn’t possible or having infinite Jetty fuel in locations with nothing but a beautiful landscape wouldn’t negatively impact others.

However, anything that goes beyond the bare minimum and/or ruins other players’ experience should result in a hardware ban—meaning that if someone wants to start a fresh account, they would need to buy a new network interface card for their PC.

In other words, all tolerable cheat-like features should already be implemented by the team, and no third-party software should be allowed.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: It's going to be difficult to interweave UE5 mechanics into Solana.  For example..  that mining demo. That's all UE5.  I would think solana minting only kicks in once the mining has stopped.  So you can't really use Solana as a safe guard in this instance . You have to rely on the game servers to make sure that everything is legit, before they fire the mint command.

I haven’t done the research, clearly, so may be way off here @Buzzec Would love to hear your thoughts on the above if you have any (and have time)

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Anti-cheat is a deep topic, but the results are pretty binary. Either it works or it doesn't. Off-the-shelf solutions are usually preferred because it's a cat and mouse game that gets very expensive to play. KYC helps with identifying people and keeping them honest but has the major downside of having to put your id in to play a video game (at least in western markets, eastern markets this is pretty standard). You also have the issue of identity fraud which is much easier once you start allowing more countries.  Our strategy for this is to be pretty reactive while architecting the system in such a way that cheating doesn't have large consequences (limits on minting, hard caps on the server side, etc.). Off-the-shelf anti-cheat is being integrated to prevent the low level cheating we saw during the surge tests.  Magic Block is cool, basically they took a solana validator and ripped out the consensus logic (~99% of the processing) making it basically a super fast transactional vm. The main issue with them is decentralization, not that we don't already have an issue with our own centralized servers though.   There are a lot of options for the "glue" between UE5 and the blockchain. A good way of lookign at it is SAGE is building the universe top down while UE5 is building bottom up. The "glue" is the middle part where they meet. It could be Magic Block with native function interaction, decentralized committee authorizations for servers, verifiable server logic, etc. For now we have a admittedly centralized solution of running the game servers with a provider and having a "trusted" authority from ATMTA.  Side Note: Hidden movement with decentralization is the hard part, specifically the forced reveal problem.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Asset ban is a hard thing, not in a technical sense but in an ethos sense. When you lock your ships in a solana program the program has full authority over them, it just so happens that we have a permission structure within our program that lets you remove the assets you locked. Logic could be built that would allow restricting assets by cheaters but then you have the problem of who decides they're a cheater? What if who decided is wrong?  I'm reminded of a quote by Justice Robert Jackson about the US Supreme Court > We are not final because we are infallible, but we are infallible only because we are final.  Blockchain ethos generally prefers freedom over control so this is a hard subject to get right. Currently we do not freeze assets for cheating in game.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: By centralization being the main issue I meant that we don't see a path to decentralization for it, they basically ripped out the decentralization to attain the speeds they have. We've kind of drawn a line on SAGE that all of it is decentralized. We've had multiple internal proposals (including some of my own) that would involve a centralized aspect to SAGE that haven't been pursued for various reasons.  Not to say we won't shift this in the future, temporary centralization is really powerful for building.

author: [Peace DAC] TGI LizardMUD
content: Thanks for the message!! However, one thing I’d like to point out is that the opposite is also true, unfortunately: if cheating is allowed in a game (or rather, if it’s not allowed but not severely punished), why should I risk investing what I could lose due to a cheater? Clearly, I’m not talking about magnetic bullets or a wall hack, but simply a soft aimbot. In competitive play, even aim assist is considered a borderline cheat, and in fact, 80% of pro players use a controller.  I agree that this is a very difficult issue to solve and that assets themselves shouldn’t be frozen, but I do think a ban on the player profile could be necessary. At the very least, it would prevent cheaters from accessing HRZ from level 0 and from having too many fleets and/or fleets that are too large.  Of course, this is just a casual comment for discussion—we’re still far from actually needing this. It’s just a friendly chat.

author: [Peace DAC] TGI LizardMUD
content:
In the Friends episode "The One with the Truth About London" (Season 7, Episode 16), a plot involves Joey hiring a "fake Joey" - an actor named Carl who resembles him - to play his identical twin for a medical study to earn extra money. This scheme of creating a false identity for financial gain parallels a common phenomenon in the space MMO game Star Atlas.
In Star Atlas, "fake identities" appear in the form of alternate accounts (often called "alts") that players create to gain economic advantages. Just as Joey tried to double his medical study earnings with a fake identity, Star Atlas players might create multiple accounts.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content: Hmm, I'm not so sure.  Take a look at this fleet: https://solscan.io/account/892e6BbJhLgqh4gHuNiMoPBXL6PU4Dw4hEcUuLG9DzQA It was rented four days ago - five seconds after it was relisted. Two other accounts were one and four seconds late and got failed txs.  If you look at the wallet that rented it, https://solscan.io/account/CDoCZYnYyykZRkqgt9T6EGRfrg2XTcmAAaKgqhujrWTP you can see that the player profile was created on the same day.  Yes, I agree. I don't think it's an exploit. They are doing what is possible in the game - all good.  But I do think that an auction (with all it's drawbacks) would be a fairer system than a FCFS and it also leads to a more realistic price.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: when you have a chance, take a look at how Entropia Universe has their Auction house setup. You can list an item at a "buy now" price, and a "minimum bid", and you put a time frame (in the game the max time-frame is 7 days). if someone lists something with a minimum bid, people have 7 days where they can do bidding wars in the hopes of getting the item at a better rate than the "buy now" price. The minute someone just uses the "buy now" option, the item is off the market.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Team implemented a new technology called "Presence" as of last week where it now knows if a player is connected, and it can refresh that players data specifically.  (Versus refreshing the entire system which can take hours.)   There was some sort of glitch Wednesday/Thursday which knocked UI refresh offline for about 8-10 hours, but since then it has been working flawlessly.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: If you are stil having issues I'd do a hard reset (f12, right click refresh button, empty cache and hard reset.)

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Also make sure you dont have a browser window running for 10+ hours.  Sometimes if i leave the window open while sleeping and then come back in the morning things are glitchy. So I'll close the browser and reload.

content: This is my account, I didn't want to make it public, any advantage is an advantage, but in this case the discussion is more important than the potential benefit. I actively follow the rental market, and I can confirm that I have not seen cases of using a bot, specifically my case with that sweet fleet - I created a new account on a hot wallet, after I could not take a single decent fleet for several days due to the need to confirm the transaction on the ledger, a hot wallet always does it faster, but this is not a bot. Currently i have 62(63 ) borrowed fleets, so i consider myself quite experienced in this aspect of the game, auction will 100% make rental healthier.  Bots, are obvious will make rental environment worse. 30-40% ROI for lending your fleet to bot is very nice business opportunity, sadly this is not true, current lowest C11 price on market: 22k$ (-crew; -parts), fair rent price: 8600atlas/day (11.5$), this is 20% ROI if you manage to lend ship for market price (with out r4 on it), with 0 delays, bot will take 50% discount, 10year investment return for buying NFT  . The auction system will always work on the lender's side, the truth is that there is no such thing as a fair price for a ship, there is only an "objectively profitable" one(for bots). I will gladly overpay for a mining ship right before the base opens, since I will compensate that costs by installing field stabilizers, which are the most expensive resource in terms of mining, I am also ready to pay 2x price of a transport ship to transfer and install the SDU on days when the other components are filled and the cost of installed SDUs flies up by 20-35%. Other example FIC selling base. As a small player i can craft contracts for months and wait till close base (1warp) will buy it, than i will borrow transport fleet, my price will be higher than "fair" price for renting this fleet for 24day daily job. The auction resolves this imbalance in favor of ship owner, which is good for overall eco system.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: Mining Score The Mining Score measures a fleet's mining efficiency relative to its rental cost. It is calculated by dividing the fleet's mining capacity by the rental price in ATLAS for the selected period. Formula: Mining Score = Mining Capacity ÷ Rental Price in ATLAS Cargo Capacity Score The Cargo Capacity Score evaluates how effective a fleet is at transporting resources. This score is determined by dividing the fleet's cargo capacity by the rental price in ATLAS. Formula: Cargo Capacity Score = Cargo Capacity ÷ Rental Price in ATLAS SDU Scanning Score The SDU Scanning Score assesses a fleet's scanning capability—critical for tasks like resource identification. It is calculated by dividing the fleet's scanning capacity by the rental price in ATLAS. Formula: SDU Scanning Score = Scanning Capacity ÷ Rental Price in ATLAS

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Not saying I have evidence, but this is one case. Doesn't necessarily dismiss the bot argument. And to be fair, if not already, it will happen eventually. Bots are part of the ecosystem. Sucks? Hell yeah. To a point where some people simply don't interact with the Global Market (as in, at all. Myself included). I'm ok with fees, I'm ok with arbitrage, margins, but I'm absolutely disgusted to my core every time the mere thought of using the Global Market surfaces. It quite literally destroyed the experience for me (and many others).  I'm not using the rental system, for many reasons, but mainly because I don't need it right now. My fleets are being used, and my operation is tight enough that I don't need more assets, unless I'm scaling up the entire operation. Which I'm not (for other reasons, not the point here). But just reading and watching what people are reporting, it gives me the same vibe I get from the Global Market, and that sucks... And I'm sure I'm not alone here.  The fact this is MVP yet, and not aimed at onboarding more people, but rather improving the system so we can have a great experience (for current and also new players/investors), should encourage us to explore all potential problems, and discuss it all, to arrive at a great product/experience. Or we are bound to reach the same experience we have with the Global Market, and that is FAR from optimal.

author: [AEP] Viktor [AEP]
content: This is my account, I didn't want to make it public, any advantage is an advantage, but in this case the discussion is more important than the potential benefit. I actively follow the rental market, and I can confirm that I have not seen cases of using a bot, specifically my case with that sweet fleet - I created a new account on a hot wallet, after I could not take a single decent fleet for several days due to the need to confirm the transaction on the ledger, a hot wallet always does it faster, but this is not a bot. Currently i have 62(63 ) borrowed fleets, so i consider myself quite experienced in this aspect of the game, auction will 100% make rental healthier.  Bots, are obvious will make rental environment worse. 30-40% ROI for lending your fleet to bot is very nice business opportunity, sadly this is not true, current lowest C11 price on market: 22k$ (-crew; -parts), fair rent price: 8600atlas/day (11.5$), this is 20% ROI if you manage to lend ship for market price (with out r4 on it), with 0 delays, bot will take 50% discount, 10year investment return for buying NFT  . The auction system will always work on the lender's side, the truth is that there is no such thing as a fair price for a ship, there is only an "objectively profitable" one(for bots). I will gladly overpay for a mining ship right before the base opens, since I will compensate that costs by installing field stabilizers, which are the most expensive resource in terms of mining, I am also ready to pay 2x price of a transport ship to transfer and install the SDU on days when the other components are filled and the cost of installed SDUs flies up by 20-35%. Other example FIC selling base. As a small player i can craft contracts for months and wait till close base (1warp) will buy it, than i will borrow transport fleet, my price will be higher than "fair" price for renting this fleet for 24day daily job. The auction resolves this imbalance in favor of ship owner, which is good for overall eco system.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
For arguments sake, lets say bots and humans both want the best deal. As such they both want the cheapest rentals most often. In the current situation there's cheap rentals because those who are lending them set the price as such. If we implement an auction, bots and humans will still have a price point at which it makes sense for them to grab a rental. We now have to incorporate any time delay from bids (if we delay 5 mins etc) in a normal auction. For those very juicy rentals this will mean potentially a long battle (bid minimums to try and get the best deal) or someone outbidding everyone and probably getting labeled as a "crazy" for paying that much for that rental.

A dutch auction is interesting since you start out at that premium if and only if the lender puts it ridiculously high in the first place. So people who are "crazy" could put some very low ball rentals out there and we will be exactly where we are today. One downside, you'd have to constantly calculate your "premium" and snag it when it reaches that eventually. There will be a delay to market valuation. Honestly I think the only way this will be fixed is if we figure out a way to find market faster and thus reducing erbodies ROI (if forced would it would be removing freedom) or add more supply.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I've spoken to Chris about a system for the local markets where it either uses the greater of price OR 7-day weighted market average to determine fee. I think if we did private contracts you would 10000% need to implement a fee based on vwap or some other factor (probably market rate) or people will bypass the current rental fees completely.

Much has already been said on the auction topic, the only thing I want to add is that the current gameplay "look at eveeye for closing rent - set an alarm - click faster than the competitor" is simply a bad gameplay that leads to unnecessary frustration and the need to create hot wallets without a ledger, abuse local markets to transfer resources and consumables from main account to hot wallet-alt acc. Also this all concerns not only "profitable" offers, you should do it every time, when you feel someone else can possible want that fleet.  The argument that a newbie won't like blocking the atlas, waiting hours for the auction to end is controversial. At least its a fair play. A newbie certainly won't like having only unfavorable offers at his disposal, because all the favorable ones are taken by experienced players with trained fingers and deep knowledge on abusing Sage

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content:
IF you use EveEye on a daily basis (which everyone here likely does), 
and you earn at least 6000 Atlas/month in profit (which most here likely do) 

you SHOULD subscribe to EveEye GoldenEye 

to pay back Risingson, who has continued to provide for us one of the most valuable tools in Star Atlas (as a hobby!).

Only 28 wallets have subscribed so far, and I think this is a SHAME.

Let's support him and everything he has done to improve our lives in SA.

AND be sure to give a   and comment on his new DAO proposal for funding. ⁠unknown

However, if ALL of us who use EveEye subscribed to GoldenEye, he may not even need DAO startup funding for very long.

This community is going to get sucked dry soon (if it's not already).

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I understand my take can be construed as controversial, it's really not. Just understand that many OGs have spent loads (some have even lost loads), to ATMTA to have a functional SAGE. It's going to be a hard ask, no matter how well intentioned, to then expect that those people should pay for a community made project. donations are fine, I feel like everything that eveeye provides should up the ante for ATMTA, but it hasn't. They need to either hire him, or find some other way to monetize him, because it's a really sour taste in the mouth for where the direction of this project is going, if we pay hand over fist first to ATMTA, then have to keep forking out $$ to every "community made project" that adds value. That's not how it works IRL, and it will never end well. It's not me being mean, it's just me being realistic. By all means, if you want to donate, donate, If I want to I will donate (and I have), but to guilt trip people about it is wrong on every level under current conditions.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I'll tell you why i'm probably bringing this up, everything eveeye provides, short of a history chart and monthly graph on LMs and GM, is what I expected from SAGE out the gate (or at the very least a couple years into the game). Now I get it that the team is focusing on UE5, and SAGE is an MVP product. But we paid for that product nonetheless (some people even losing money with paying for it), caveat due to market conditions. It's a pretty sour taste in the mouth, when we paid already for something, that we expect to have all these features, only for a community made project to trump all those expectations, then monetize. I get it, we don't have to use it, just a really sour taste. That's the whole point. Nothing against donations for hobbies, honestly, happy to leave it at that. But if risingson is looking for fulltime compensation, because he wants to focus on eveeye fulltime, he might not get that from donations. That means monetizing. Where does that come from ? the community. This community is getting sucked drier by the day, there won't be any $$ left soon if things continue in this vein, that is what I am trying to warn against, and that is where I am coming from.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: only whales would pay $10/month for a community made tool. people looking to try out the game would scoff at the idea. If he wanted to incorporate an "ATLAS prime" type function, to essentially be able to use eveeye to perform tx on chain, I would think that is a much fairer usecase. of course, this is not stable, because maybe nobody will use that feature (and can you blame them after everything i've said), AFAIK, ATOM charges over ATMTA fees, SLYA not there yet (I find SLYA to be the most valuable community made tool to date). I dunno what the solution is, because I do agree that these developers should be rewarded, I just don't know how great it's going to go down, if all of a sudden anything relevant is locked behind a paywall.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
Just a quick clarification: every tool has its pros and cons. Let’s take the two you mentioned as an example:

SLY is perfect for those who want to handle everything on their own, from a single machine. It’s affordable, but it comes with the downsides of self-custody: you need to keep your PC running, maintain a stable internet connection, deal with power consumption—things that aren’t always visible but still add up.

ATOM Cloud, on the other hand, provides all of that for an explicit cost (Atom core is free). You don’t need to pay for electricity or internet on your end, and you get higher fault tolerance as part of the package.

What I can say is that yes, there’s still a paywall—there’s no way around it. I know you’re implying that all these tools should have been part of SAGE natively, and I could agree. But they’re not, and we have to deal with the reality as it is.

We’ve already made it through the darkest hour—we can’t give up now, just moments before dawn!

Things are coming together, automation is already planned inside the game natively as Danny said some time ago, I guess detailed information is being worked on as well, everyone will have access to that natively, even without paying anyone!

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: But I want to say I see your point, I’m playing devil’s advocate here because, as you said, it’s a very hard question

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I'm doing a little research into the modding aspect of EVE frontier, and it's not an exact comparison. The modders in EVE frontier are not actually creating something new, they are just expounding on a toolset that is already provided by CCP, to create things within the world of EVE frontier. As they are not actually changing anything in base gameplay, base gameplay just works already.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: this kind of modding is more like a product, and for the modders to charge for their product as a one time cost, is totally acceptable. I guess the painpoint (and I mentioned this above), is that the "mods" we currently have are all very "live service" mods (many of which that without them, SAGE is nigh unplayable sometimes), so it's not entirely shocking they are looking for more fixed income in the form of subscriptions, or what have you. I just don't know if that's the best way to proceed with game development ethos, seems like anything live-service related, if you must charge for it, should be a subscription model for a feature set that should come with the game at base level. Any "add-ons" can be sold as "products" at a one time cost to the player/user.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
Interesting discussion, if we abstract from SA, I think that modding community is a what separates a good game from a great one. I still believe that the best years of Blizzard were exactly when, together with the game (Warcraft, for example), they released a powerful map editor, community members made Tower Defense, Dota etc which became 100 times more popular than original RTS game. Initially, battle royales were mods that overtook the original games in popularity. The community will always do better than the team, simply because the community is 1000 passioned full-time players, and the team are 10 full-time developers and half-time players. I am very glad that our team shares this vision and does not try to monopolize the development process. Sage is not perfect, but it works and I do not think it is correct to compare Sage with eveeye or Sly.
It's hard to call me a fanboy, but in this case I want to be the team's advocate, let me remind you that Sage was developed as a mini-game so that players had smth before the release of the main functionality. In the process, this mini-game turned into a living unique economy in which many of us spend a lot of time, and some even pay their bills with Sage. Do not forget that Sage, unlike Sly or Eveeye, is not only an interface for mining/scanning, but also a huge block for processing the blockchain with its own security, autonomy, etc., this is not a browser game made on the knee for selling loot boxes, millions $ of assets involved

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: If we can get eveeye, and SLYA to work altogether in one tool, and I never have to interact with SAGE, I will gladly pay a subscription for that model. and I would hope that close to 100% (if not 100%) of any monetization would go to the developers of those tools.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: The whole idea of introducing community modding, and monetizing it, would be to create a competitive environment, not just between modders, but also as a yardstick for the dev team themselves. if we end up just paying for everything, because it's all disjointed, i don't think that has a good outcome, there has to be some streamlining somewhere.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: is it bots? or people with hot wallets and not pressing 6 times on ledger   it doesn't make sense to me that bots are renting. like for what? they can't re-rent it. sure, maybe someone has a bot because they need a lot of miners and want to rent specific fleets... but i think someone being faster on the button press makes more sense

reply.preview: is it bots? or people with hot wallets and not pressing 6 times on ledger it doesn't make sense to me that bots are renting. like for what? they can't re-rent it. sure, maybe someone has a bot because they need a lot of miners and want to rent specific fleets... but i think someone being faster on the button press makes more sense

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: We do know that ATOM has notifications for new fleet rentals.  So I suspect that these notifications+human playing is speeding up the process.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: We also know that some very good deals are going through. These are likely otc and preplanned so people are ready to click as soon as the fleet is put up for rental.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: I also think that people are underestimating the complexity of automating rentals.  It requires an economic component to make a decision on whether a rental is good or not.  That's not easily automated.   Further, you can't automate placement in Sage of these rental fleets. It must be done manually.  And, most fleets require some sort of balance and sophisticated operations to achieve a high ROI.  If you start renting every ship you will destroy that balance.  It's really not feasible.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: So any "bots" looking to automate in any sustained fashion will likely be mining hydrogen which has the lowest ROI.  Which means their economic calculations need to be even tighter.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: it's very easy to know if a fleet is worth renting or not (for a bot to be scripted) using mining rate + cargo capacity. As to the question of why would it be worth for a bot to rent ? well, behind every great bot is a great human being. That human could very well be playing the game.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: if there were evidence that no bot is automating rentals (which is equally as hard to prove as that there are bots), i couldn't care less. But that is not the case.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: people would use bots if they had them, and people will make them if they haven't already. this whole debate on whether or not people are doing it is weird

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I challenge anyone to try and snatch anyone of these rentals without bot assistance, and lmk how it goes (if they don't have crew, then probably out of bot range).

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: the exact same debate was going on for months when galactic market started. it wasn't until bots were clearly dominating that everyone was in agreement that it was happening. what the point? clearly there's money to be made, and people will bot it, once they do it wont matter how fast you can calculate the value, or click the buttons. Bots will dominate. whether its happening yet or not is meaningless. the only way it wont be a thing is if the on chain program changes, or the project dies

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: Honestly, i'd give more credit if people would just say "yeah, there's bots, so what ?"

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: but suggesting there is only human interaction in rentals is, as you said, absurd.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: but suggesting there is only human interaction in rentals is, as you said, absurd.

reply.preview: but suggesting there is only human interaction in rentals is, as you said, absurd.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: the important thing is that it will happen, i don't think many people will disagree with that, so the debate becomes whether or not is it acceptable or if we need to make a change to prevent / mitigate.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: unless everyone has access to a bot, on rentals, there are no tools for non-bot users to combat a bot. It's evident, that to level the playing field, a human needs to be able to price a bot out of its range, something which is at least possible on the GM.

author: defiantwyvern
content:
the real question that should be answered is what is best for the game in terms of how players get rentals. Bots or not doesn't matter that much, b/c there will always be fast players. Like people sniping good buy-it-now deals on ebay.

IMO auctions would be great for players that aren't familiar with the system, and they can rent out their fleet to any willing participant (bot or not) at a fair market value without knowing anything.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
I've thought about this pretty heavily, and the amount of development work and testing required to ensure a decent experience seems like little gain when the argument is that "we don't get good deals". I have a few other features I'd like to pursue before an auction (which I'd prefer to see how market finds it self over time as well).

For example,
1) allow rental transfers between subprofiles
2) dac rentals
3) windowed intervals instead of 0000 UTC epochs
4) cancellations with custom delay (cancel now, X hours up to 24)

author: defiantwyvern
content:
If you are talking about voting to the raise the price, I think an auction is the real form of that system. People raising the price are taking stake in the raise.

Could an auction be implemented as such on chain. Just wondering about why people say it's complicated to do on chain.

bidders put their bid into an account (tied to the rental auction account), sets the current time time on the rental auction account + the current max bid.,bidders can remove their bid (reclaim their atlas) if they are not the highest bid.,the highest bidder (or rental supplier) can start the rental if no new bid has occurred in 5 minutes.,
are there other complications?

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
No system will fix it against bots, it's a reality (wether it already exists or not). Bots will always exist, and fill the spread.

My personal point on this: they absolutely ruin the experience to the Average Joe user. And even to some hardcore users. Like myself, for example. I simply refuse to use the Galactic Marketplace at all, now. Avoid it like the plague.
As for rentals, I looked into it, and came to the conclusion that it's not for me. I'd rather own the assets. But I really wanna see mechanisms do simply render bots useless. Private rentals would just kill this whole debate. Negotiate, rent, fuck bots/scalpers.
And adjust the fee system to prevent exploiting (make fees a % of VWAP, done).

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
As always, I was late to the conversation, Honestly, I did not fully understand the system that @[AEP] Bodhitree @[SLY] anthonyra discussed, perhaps this is an argument that the system is not obvious (or i am not smart enough) and we do not need to complicate the game (Sage) that is already extremely difficult to enter for new players.
Also, I might have missed, were there any technical arguments against the system with hidden bids?
It seems to me that the ideal auction looks like this: rent deals take place every 12 (6) hours (exact time. like epochs in SAGE). Good for players from different time zones. New rent, posted less than 1 hour before the moment of transaction, is transferred to the next epoch (to prevent OTC/sniping), players place hidden bids (1 bid from 1 player, so that there is no spam and overpayments for commissions), the highest bid wins the fleet. If you do not want to freeze your atlas, you can simply take payment after auction is closed. If wallet has not enough balance - the next price is triggered and so on until the transaction is closed. 

Allow the owner set a minimum daily amount/minimum number of rental days, the auction will do the rest

In the long term, it would be cool to allow bidding on already taken fleets, so at the end of the lease the fleet does not return to the market, but remains with the borrower/passes to a new one at a higher price, if rent is not cancelled by the owner, of course. This will solve the problem of "downtime" for ship owners associated with a delay during the auction.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Nothing is hidden on the blockchain, a system like this would need to happen off chain or you need to leverage some fancy tech which will add complexity.  From an explain it like I'm 5 standpoint, right now we have people (bots/humans) sniping rentals that are under market valuation (good deals). My suggestion at a system to try would be a burn to increment rate feature.  You'll be able to see currently rented out fleets, at which point you can see the current set rate for that rental. If you see a rental for example listed at 100 ATLAS/day and the max duration posssible is 24 days. You would be able to increment that rate by 1 ATLAS/day by burning 24 ATLAS. It essentially allows you to force the rental rate up at the cost of burning ATLAS instead of waiting on the lender to cancel the rental and re-list it.  Some limitations in place would be, you can only do this while it's actively being rented and you're capped at 2x the current rental rate (ie 100 ATLAS/day) in this example. Therefore, you could or the community together could increase that rentals rate to 200 ATLAS/day for 2400 ATLAS (1 full rental cycle) during that current active rental.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
My suggestion is probably the closet I would get to an auction. It "may" sound complex but its only one instruction added to the program and doesn't require people to escrow funds for X amount of time. It allows people to help lenders get to market valuation but not so much so that someone could out price everyone but their rental. Unless they want to pay for it.

If you don't want to implement a burn then this is a moot point. Just would be easy to implement, gets rentals to market valuation faster and makes the supply deflationary.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
Some clarification, right now if a lender wants to increase their rate they have to cancel to ensure someone doesn’t rent again at the low rate. Not ideal.

The program does support the lender being able to update their rate while having an active rental but the UI doesn’t support it yet.

Step 1 should ideally be that.

However in this scenario, someone sees a cheap rental go to someone for 24 days. They hope the lender sees that they should increase the rate and they  should. But you can’t naturally force this. Just have to hope they see it.

Implementing this would allow anyone to force increase a rentals rate by burning ATLAS. So that it’s not 100% free and thus helping prevent abuse. In this case abuse is I have an IMP and I’ll force increase all other IMPs to make mine the cheapest. (Sure do that but it’ll cost you 24 ATLAS per 1 increment thus costing 24k ATLAS per rental to increment theirs 1k). If you do that to others they could also force increase yours to match theirs (again for 24k though)

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: And the VWAP option isn't as clear cut as it sounds. First of all, it disconnects the economics of a rental from the free market. Perhaps somebody genuinely wants to offer their fleet at some minor discount so it rents faster (ignoring the exploiters for this example). They would take a greater hit on income because we're forcing a minimum. Separately, determining the appropriate fee based on VWAP indicates we need to be defining the appropriate valuerental price of all combinations of assets.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: My complaining (and many others') is not towards the crazy best deals. It's against having any opportunity. Maybe rentals just isn't for me... Or maybe I'm the example on how we're missing out on opportunities. Same line of thought on Galactic Marketplace. Doing nothing worked out? Maybe. It's there. But I'm not there. At all. And many others aren't. We pushed people away from an entire branch of the economy. And we're doing the same with rentals.  It's missed revenue. And that's a HUGE opportunity cost. Especially when rentals generate revenue for 3rd parties involved with the program. If we shift the discussion, and ask all these 3rd parties if they'd want a system that attracts more users, and generate more $ in fees, what would be their answer? I know what it would be.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Thx for clarification,  i think this is interesting idea, i would support it, but im a nerd, most players would prefer to find fleet - give best price - take fleet. Participating in pricing ships they will not get seems a little weird.  Talking on main topic you all focus on "profitable fleets" but current main problem is liquidity. I have to fight for every normal fleet that coming to market. But If tomorrow all 75m wwap will be listed i will still have to show my fast fingers technique because some fleets would be better then other.  My main complaint is the current mechanics and unfairness of the process.  I dont want to be the fastest of those who rob the owner of the fleet i want to give him best price. I also don't understand why @SW4GN3R  is so insistent on instant rent in a strategy game with very slow gameplay. Mb it will matter when combat comes, but if sniping either by fast players or bots will still be a thing this will make people even more mad watching how your opponent from other faction stills fleet you wanted to grab to be able defend yourself   Last but not least, those cheap fleets making whole rental market worse. They dumping price making ship lord business less profitable. The auction will work on the lender side driving price up or at least keep it fair.  It is sad that the auction with hidden bids is difficult to implement other options look worse, but still much better then current system.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: No worries it’s all brainstorming, I think it’s an issue in most places that profits exist just look at Taylor Swift tickets and scalpers.  I’m cooking up a potential GBM implementation but the issue is the final highest bid. The current option is that the highest bidder will sit there until they’re outbid or the contract is closed. (Better than burning the Atlas) but Solana makes it hard to auto renew from a different state account. I’m afraid the added complexity would and could cause unwanted bugs. Since we’d have contracts with GBM enabled and some that doesn’t and Solana doesn’t like it most times.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content: Because you’re looking at it from the perspective of an existing player with assets already in use. Which, I definitely want existing players to find value in the platform. I also want new people to see potential in becoming an asset owner just for passive income potential. But beyond that, I want potential new players to see this as a low (financial) barrier entry point. If their first experience is waiting hours or days just to access a fleet because it’s an auction process, they’re not going to stick around. Even if the platform and even the product aren’t entirely in a place to accommodate that last profile today, I don’t want to modify it now in a way that ruins for them later.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I agree with the narrative, it will work out in perfect conditions with infinity good priced fleets, but I cant imagine how a new player with minimal investment in crew will be able to snatch a good offer, there are too many unknown nuances that we, the current players, have learned over the past year and a half. A newbie will always get scraps until he becomes a sufficiently experienced player, and he will only become one gradually immersing himself in the game. Free sage from Chris is the answer here, but after completing it and playing with rent mechanics there he will already be a full-fledged participant in the market and auction wont be a problem.  I myself am a great example of what you want to achieve with new players, i was able to grab 3 good fleets(2 a bit above avg + 1 juicy) on MUD side(new wallet), added 1000 crew to that account and grinding around 35-40k atlas daily, i will return my 5k$ crew investment next in next 3.5month which is insane opportunity keeping in mind current altas rate, but again i outplayed at least 4 experienced MUD players to achieve this and i am crafting fics with optimal roots, im also using market for sdu and all tools like sly/eve, this is not what new player will do 5 min after he created wallet.

reply.preview: I agree with the narrative, it will work out in perfect conditions with infinity good priced fleets, but I cant imagine how a new player with minimal investment in crew will be able to snatch a good offer, there are too many unknown nuances that we, the current players, have learned over the past year and a half. A newbie will always get scraps until he becomes a sufficiently experienced player, and he will only become one gradually immersing himself in the game. Free sage from Chris is the answer here, but after completing it and playing with rent mechanics there he will already be a full-fledged participant in the market and auction wont be a problem. author: DominicVainMod
content: personally my view is that bots monopolizing high margin opportunities in any facet of the game is a bad thing. like what we see in the ship markets. either tools need to made publicly available like in sage, or the underlying contract needs adjustment.  My point is that arguing over whether or not there are currently bots in the rental market is a waste of time. no one can argue that there are high margin opportunities in rentals, and it's silly to think anyone can physically compete with an optimized sniper bot even if they are on the pc 24/7. So the discussion should be around whether or not that is a problem long term, if it's worth solving, and if so how would it be done. debating over when its going to happen if not already is pointless. it will happen for sure given enough time with the current contract.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: There’s so many variables with bots.  Is it better to have bots for price appreciation? Liquidity?  Is the frustration of dealing with bots to community members a greater risk than the perceived benefits?  Is there something you can actually do?   Funnily enough, Pokemon has a huge bot problem and is currently witnessing the greatest bull market it’s ever had. But the normal collectors are not happy that product is 2x-3x more expensive than they’re used to. It’s somewhat similar.  These zillion dollar companies haven’t solved bots which makes me think.   At this point I’m just here for the conversation because I think it’s interesting, appreciate your thoughts brother!

author: DominicVainMod
content: i think bots in general can be positive for the reasons you listed, but steps should be made to prevent them from monopolizing high margin opportunities. if they are able to just take the cream of the crop off the top that's not helping anyone.   the optics itself is a risk. at this point the bots in the GM will even accept little to low margin just to disincentive people from attempting to compete. that said there maybe more important endeavors so ideally the solution is a low lift. the time aspect is also relevant. gm bots are a far bigger problem now that the big ones have made hundreds of thousands of dollars and have that liquidity available to dominate. that and they've had plenty of time to optimize. they've even got spoof accounts that give the impression of a competitive price by having one bot set a floor and their other bot make a better bid. as well as separate bots for the larger ships to accommodate various perimeters for the different markets.   there are countless things you can do. imo the most effective solutions are built into the contract. for example simply saying "its against the rules to bot" is a terrible solution.  margins are another consideration. the GM offered relatively high margins to bots for years now. the result is basically a permanent bot whale that will be a ball and chain on the marketplace for the foreseeable future. even if you built a bot today that was effective you'd be unable to compete with the guy who's made probably a million dollars by now. not only has he had time to build in anti bot mechanics but his margins are insurmountable basically at this point.

author: ROME | Macalloyus ⪛⦿⫺
content: Because you’re looking at it from the perspective of an existing player with assets already in use. Which, I definitely want existing players to find value in the platform. I also want new people to see potential in becoming an asset owner just for passive income potential. But beyond that, I want potential new players to see this as a low (financial) barrier entry point. If their first experience is waiting hours or days just to access a fleet because it’s an auction process, they’re not going to stick around. Even if the platform and even the product aren’t entirely in a place to accommodate that last profile today, I don’t want to modify it now in a way that ruins for them later.

author: Narr_nEoNONI
content:
Hi, There was an interesting conversation about rentals!

To add to my thoughts, I don't think the private rental system can solve the problems perfectly that people are talking about.

However, there are several reasons why private rental systems should be launched quickly.

It can be the core content of the guild system that will become active in the future.,
It provides opportunities for new users who flow in through current users.New users who don't have ships, can't get a reasonable fleet of rents in the current market (idk rental bot exists),It's actually a problem my numerous community members who's interested in star atlas is having. Lending him a fleet at reasonable rent through direct rental can create new users.,,
I don't know why fees are a problem. Is the ultimate purpose of the ship rental system profitable for developers and ATMTA? What kind of problems does it cause to rent TreeArrow for 100 ATLAS? 
(This is not a problem at all, at least as long as the private rental system is implemented.)

I hope the rental system develops quickly  

Have a nice weekend

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: Well, I don’t have anything against the title—I never have. But as I’ve always said, it’s just “more of the same”: a pretty anonymous battle royale with a Web3 flavor.  I really liked the "detaching legs" mechanic—that was a cool idea—but that’s pretty much it. Third-person shooters just aren’t competitive by nature. If you’re aiming for a competitive experience, third-person isn't the way to go. Even Fortnite was more about building than actual shooting in its "Prime Time".  OTG did well as long as the team paid streamers to bring it on Twitch—and that was basically it. If it were a genuinely good title with real audience interest, some streamers would have stuck with it. But if you check Twitch right now the game barely cracks 1k viewers.  Just look at this interest chart… it’s brutal.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: I tried using the end of December  as starting date to trim the outliers—it's not super accurate, I'm sorry. Btw assuming this is an already published game, I don't like these graphs for two main reasons:  There's a lack of weekly cyclicality, which is unusual for this type of game. (maybe its only due to low number of players) There are no visible spikes that usually come from in-game events (I'm ignorant here XD: are there even any events like that happening in the game?)  This is just my opinion anyway and I'm saying its "dead" from a mainstream point of view, I still wish them all the best for the niche they created!
